stem: A circle has radius \( [[r]] \). An arc of the circle subtends a central angle of \( \dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{6} \) radians. What is the length of the arc?
solution: Arc length equals radius times the central angle in radians: \( s = r\theta = [[r]]\cdot\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{6} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the sector-area formula \( \frac{1}{2}r^2\theta \) instead of the arc-length formula.
This doubles the correct arc length.
This drops the factor of \( \pi \), forgetting the angle is in radians (a pure number times \( \pi \)).
This doubles the angle before applying the formula.
This adds an unrelated amount to the correct arc length.
This halves the correct arc length.

stem: A circle has radius \( [[r]] \). What is the length of an arc that subtends a central angle of \( [[deg]]^{\circ} \)?
solution: The arc is the fraction \( \frac{[[deg]]}{360} \) of the full circumference \( 2\pi r \): \( 2\pi([[r]])\cdot\dfrac{[[deg]]}{360} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the full circle's area formula instead of its circumference.
This divides by 180 instead of 360, doubling the fraction of the circle used.
This forgets the factor of \( 2\pi \), treating the arc length as a bare fraction of the radius.
This reports the full circumference, ignoring the fraction of the circle.
This adds an unrelated amount to the correct arc length.
This halves the correct arc length.

stem: A circular sector has radius \( [[r]] \) and central angle \( \dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{6} \) radians. What is the area of the sector?
distractor_rationales: This uses the arc-length formula \( r\theta \) instead of the sector-area formula.
This forgets the \( \frac{1}{2} \) factor in the sector-area formula.
This uses \( r \) instead of \( r^2 \) in the formula.
This doubles the angle before applying the formula.
This adds an unrelated amount to the correct area.
This doubles the correct area.

stem: A circular sector has radius \( [[r]] \) and central angle \( [[deg]]^{\circ} \). What is the area of the sector?
solution: The sector is the fraction \( \frac{[[deg]]}{360} \) of the full circle's area \( \pi r^2 \): \( \pi([[r]])^2\cdot\dfrac{[[deg]]}{360} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the circumference formula instead of the area formula.
This divides by 180 instead of 360, doubling the fraction of the circle used.
This forgets the factor of \( \pi \).
This reports the full circle's area, ignoring the fraction used.
This adds an unrelated amount to the correct area.
This doubles the correct area.

stem: An arc of a circle with radius \( [[r]] \) has length \( [[arclen]] \). What is the measure of the central angle it subtends, in radians?
solution: Since \( s = r\theta \), the central angle is \( \theta = \dfrac{s}{r} = \dfrac{[[arclen]]}{[[r]]} = [[answer]] \) radians.
distractor_rationales: This multiplies by \( r \) instead of dividing by it.
This mixes the sector-area formula's factor into a length relationship.
This doubles the correct angle.
This halves the correct angle.
This adds an unrelated amount to the correct angle.
This adds an unrelated amount to the correct angle.

stem: A central angle of a circle measures \( [[central]]^{\circ} \). An inscribed angle subtends the same arc as this central angle. What is the measure, in degrees, of the inscribed angle?
solution: The Inscribed Angle Theorem says an inscribed angle is half of the central angle subtending the same arc: \( \dfrac{[[central]]}{2} = [[answer]] \)\(^{\circ}\).
distractor_rationales: This reports the central angle itself instead of halving it.
This doubles the central angle instead of halving it.
This uses the supplementary angle instead of the Inscribed Angle Theorem.
This divides by 3 instead of 2.
This adjusts the correct angle by an unrelated amount.
This adjusts the correct angle by an unrelated amount.

stem: Two chords of a circle intersect inside the circle. The two arcs intercepted by the pair of vertical angles at the intersection point measure \( [[arc1]]^{\circ} \) and \( [[arc2]]^{\circ} \). What is the measure, in degrees, of the angle formed at the intersection?
solution: The angle formed by two chords intersecting inside a circle equals half the SUM of the two intercepted arcs: \( \dfrac{[[arc1]]+[[arc2]]}{2} = [[answer]] \)\(^{\circ}\).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 2, using the raw sum of the two arcs.
This uses the external-angle (difference) formula instead of the internal-angle (sum) formula.
This takes the absolute difference of the arcs rather than half their sum.
This only halves one of the two arcs before adding.
This adjusts the correct angle by an unrelated amount.
This adjusts the correct angle by an unrelated amount.

stem: Two secants drawn from a point outside a circle intercept arcs of measure \( [[far]]^{\circ} \) (far arc) and \( [[near]]^{\circ} \) (near arc). What is the measure, in degrees, of the angle formed at the external point?
solution: The angle formed by two secants meeting outside a circle equals half the DIFFERENCE of the intercepted arcs: \( \dfrac{[[far]]-[[near]]}{2} = [[answer]] \)\(^{\circ}\).
distractor_rationales: This uses the internal-angle (sum) formula instead of the external-angle (difference) formula.
This forgets to divide by 2, using the raw difference of the two arcs.
This computes the difference with the wrong sign before halving.
This only halves the far arc before subtracting the near arc.
This adjusts the correct angle by an unrelated amount.
This adjusts the correct angle by an unrelated amount.

stem: A circular segment is cut off by a chord of a circle with radius \( [[r]] \), where the chord subtends a central angle of \( [[theta]] \) radians. What is the area of the segment (the region between the chord and the arc)?
solution: The segment area is the sector area minus the triangle area: \( \frac{1}{2}r^2(\theta - \sin\theta) = \frac{1}{2}([[r]])^2\left([[theta]] - \sin([[theta]])\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the full sector area, forgetting to subtract the triangular piece.
This reports just the triangle's area, forgetting the sector.
This forgets the \( \frac{1}{2} \) factor.
This adds the triangle area back instead of subtracting it.
This adjusts the correct area by an unrelated amount.
This doubles the correct area.
